pattern: Counting arrangements under an adjacency rule is a Hamiltonian-path count, which collapses to a bitmask DP over (visited set, last element) instead of enumerating permutations.
hints: Think of each number as a node and draw an edge between two numbers when one divides the other; a special permutation is exactly an ordering that walks every node using only those edges.

Count those orderings with a bitmask DP: dp[mask][last] = number of ways to have placed exactly the elements in mask, ending on index last, built by extending dp[mask without last][prev] whenever prev and last are connected.

Initialize dp[1<<i][i] = 1 for every i, transition by trying every unvisited next index whose value shares a divisibility relation with the current last, and sum dp[full_mask][*] modulo 1e9+7 for the answer.

edge_inputs.description: two elements where one divides the other, both orderings are special
two coprime elements, no ordering is special
four pairwise-coprime primes, no adjacency edges exist at all
a value of 1 divides everything, but that alone can't bridge more than two other pairwise-unrelated numbers into one path
max-length chain of powers of two, every pair is divisibility-related so every permutation is special
values near the upper bound, a divisor chain of three large numbers
two disjoint divisibility-related pairs with no edge between the pairs, so no full ordering exists
a chain of powers of three, fully divisibility-connected

stress.description: 14 distinct large random values, typically a sparse divisibility graph
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = 14
    nums = rng.sample(range(1, 1000000000), n)
    return [nums]

stress.description: 14 values forming a total-order chain (random base times increasing powers of two), a fully-connected divisibility graph
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = 14
    base = rng.randint(1, 1000)
    nums = [base * (2 ** i) for i in range(n)]
    return [nums]
